content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 12, 1976


Hon. Leo T. McCarthy
Speaker, California Assembly
State Capitol, Room 3164
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Conflict of Interest Code
for Assembly Staff

Dear Assemblyman McCarthy:

In early April of this year, we discussed the applicability of the Political Reform Act, which requires adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code by all state agencies, to the California Legislature. At that time, the propriety of the requirement of submission of the legislative Codes to the Commission for review and approval was raised. You urged the Commission to seek an Attorney General's opinion on the constitutional issue raised. We agreed to do so.

To allow time for receipt and review of the opinion, and without prejudice to the merits of any issue raised by the Legislature, the Commission extended the deadline for submission of a Conflict of Interest Code by the Legislature. In the meanwhile, Senate Concurrent Resolution 106, which embodies a Conflict of Interest Code for officers and employees of the Legislature, was introduced and passed by the Legislature. This Code substantially complies with the requirements of the Act, and legislative staff, identified as "designated employees", are now filing disclosure statements pursuant to the provisions of the Resolution.

On September 17, 1976, the Attorney General issued Opinion No. CV 76/105 (copy attached) concluding that:

1. The Legislature is an "agency" within the meaning of Government Code section 87300 and must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code.

2. However, the Legislature is not required to submit its Conflict of Interest Code to the Fair Political Practices Commission for approval pursuant to Government Code section 87303.

Hon. Leo T. McCarthy
October 12, 1976                                          Page Two


     In light of the above circumstances, the Commission is
deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of
state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes
to the Commission for review and approval. To insure that
your staff has the latest Commission regulations, policy
statements, and other materials relating to Conflict of
Interest Code development, however, we have provided Fred
Taugher with the attached informational pamphlet on Conflict
of Interest Codes, along with a copy of the above-referenced
Attorney General's Opinion.

     If we can be of further assistance to you or your staff
on matters involving conflicts of interest, please feel free
to contact us.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Edgar A. Kery
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division


EAK:glb
Enclosures

cc: Fred Taugher
    Consultant
    Assembly Rules Committee

87300

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 12, 1976

Mr. John Williams
Auditor General
925 L Street
Suite 750
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Conflict of Interest Code
for Office of Auditor General

Dear Mr. Williams:

In early April of this year, we discussed, with the Speaker
of the Assembly and the President Pro Tem of the Senate, the
applicability of the Political Reform Act, which requires adop-
tion of a Conflict of Interest Code by all state agencies, to
the California Senate and Assembly. At that time, the propriety
of the Act requiring submission of the legislative codes to the
Commission for review and approval was raised. We agreed to
seek an Attorney General's opinion on the constitutional issue
raised.

In the meanwhile, Senate Concurrent Resolution 106, which
embodies a Conflict of Interest Code for officers and employees
of the Legislature, including the Office of the Auditor General,
was introduced and passed by the Legislature. This Code substan-
tially complies with the requirements of the Act, and legislative
staff, including staff of the Auditor General, identified as
"designated employees", are now filing disclosure statements
pursuant to the provisions of this Resolution.

On July 15, 1976, the Legislative Counsel of California, in
an opinion requested by Assemblyman Mike Cullen, Chairman of the
Joint Legislative Audit Committee of the Legislature, stated that
the Office of the Auditor General need not submit a Conflict of
Interest Code to the Fair Political Practices Commission. The
Opinion stated that:

The Office of the Auditor General is under the
control of the Joint Legislative Audit Committee
(Section 1050 et seq.), and the Auditor General and
his staff are employees of the Committee (Section
10504). The office is supported from the Contingent

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 327-5640 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-6001 • ENFORCEMENT DIVISION OF DISTRICT OFFICES AND STATE

Mr. John Williams
October 12, 1976                                          Page Two


     Funds of the Assembly and Senate in the same
manner as other Joint Committees of the Leg-
islature (Section 10504). Thus, any discus-
sion of the Legislature's duty to submit a
Conflict of Interest Code is equally appli-
cable to the Office of the Auditor General.


     On September 17, 1976, the Attorney General issued Opinion
No. CV 76/105 (copy attached) concluding that:

     1.  The Legislature is an "agency" within
the meaning of Government Code section 87300
and must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code.

     2.  However, the Legislature is not required
to submit its Conflict of Interest Code to the
Fair Political Practices Commission for approval
pursuant to Government Code section 87303.

     In light of the above circumstances, the Commission is
deleting the California Senate and Assembly and the Office of
the Auditor General from its list of state agencies required
to submit Conflict of Interest Codes to the Commission for
review and approval. To insure that your staff has the latest
Commission regulations, policy statements and other materials
relating to Conflict of Interest Code development, however, we
enclose an informational packet on Conflict of Interest Codes,
along with a copy of the above-referenced Attorney General's
Opinion.

     If we can be of further assistance to you or your staff
on matters involving conflicts of interest, please feel free
to contact us.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Edgar A. Kerry
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division

EAK:glb
Enclosure

cc:  Hon. Mike Cullen
     Chairman, Joint Legislative
     Audit Committee of the Legislature

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 12, 1976


Hon. James R. Mills
Chairman, Rules Committee
California State Senate
State Capitol, Room 5100
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Conflict of Interest
Code for Senate Staff

Dear Senator Mills:

In early April of this year, we discussed the applicability
of the Political Reform Act, which requires adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code by all state agencies, to the Legislature.
At that time the propriety of the Act requiring submission of the
legislative Codes to the Commission for review and approval was
raised. For further guidance in this matter, the Commission
agreed to seek an Attorney General's opinion on the constitutional issue raised.

To allow time for receipt and review of the opinion and
without prejudice to any issue raised by the Legislature, the
Commission extended the deadline for submission of a Conflict
of Interest Code by the Legislature. In the meantime, Senate
Concurrent Resolution 106, which embodies a Conflict of Interest
Code for officers and employees of the Legislature, was introduced and passed by the Legislature. This code substantially
complies with the requirements of the Act, and legislative
staff, identified as "designated employees", are now filing
disclosure statements pursuant to the provisions of the
Resolution.

On September 17, 1976, the Attorney General issued Opinion
No. CV 76/105 (copy attached) concluding that:

1. The Legislature is an "agency" within the
meaning of Government Code section 87300 and must
adopt a Conflict of Interest Code.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-6011 • ENFORCEMENT COUNSEL OF INTEREST 322-1886

Hon. James R. Mills
October 12, 1976
Page Two


2. However, the Legislature is not required
to submit its Conflict of Interest Code to the Fair
Political Practices Commission for approval pursuant
to Government Code section 87303.

In light of the above circumstances, the Commission is
deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of
state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes
to the Commission for review and approval. To insure that
your staff has the latest Commission regulations, policy
statements, and other materials relating to Conflict of
Interest Code development, however, we have provided John
Williamson with the attached informational packet on Conflict
of Interest Codes, along with a copy of the above-referenced
Attorney General's Opinion.

If we can be of further assistance to you or your staff
on matters involving conflicts of interest, please feel free
to contact us.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest
Division


EAK:glb
Enclosure

cc: John C. Williamson
    Executive Officer
    Senate Rules Committee

sections.conclusion: The Commission is deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes to the Commission for review and approval, based on the Attorney General's opinion that while the Legislature is an 'agency' that must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit such code to the FPPC for approval.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the California Legislature and legislative agencies (Assembly, Senate, and Office of the Auditor General) must submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for review and approval under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Based on Attorney General Opinion No. CV 75/105, the FPPC concluded that while the Legislature must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit that code to the FPPC for approval or review. Therefore, the Commission removed the California Senate, Assembly, and the Office of the Auditor General from its list of agencies required to submit codes for FPPC review.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of three related advice letters with similar structure and conclusion. No formal Q/A section headers, but the substance and purpose are clear. Text quality varies with some OCR errors. Multiple recipients but same core message. Letters reference attached Attorney General Opinion No. CV 75/105 for authority.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the California Legislature and legislative agencies (Assembly, Senate, and Office of the Auditor General) must submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for review and approval under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Commission is deleting the California Senate and Assembly from its list of state agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes to the Commission for review and approval, based on the Attorney General's opinion that while the Legislature is an 'agency' that must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, it is not required to submit such code to the FPPC for approval.

embedding.summary: This consists of three October 12, 1976 FPPC correspondence letters to the Speaker of the Assembly, the Auditor General, and the Senate Rules Committee Chairman clarifying that the Legislature and its related agencies are not required to submit their Conflict of Interest Codes to the FPPC for approval, following an Attorney General's constitutional opinion on the matter.
